acceptance_targets: [chapter_14_f001] PASS if the revised language at `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:49`, `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:333`, and `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:391` is recognizably new on the page while preserving the same dramatic function; FAIL if a reader can still point to those spans as verbatim or obvious near-verbatim reuse of style-bible example lines.

[chapter_14_f002] PASS if the revised spans at `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:285-321` and `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:389-417` still deliver the corruption facts and job threat but no longer read as a run of back-to-back quotable lines, with at least two visible leverage shifts caused by Nate's pushback or Victor's deflection; FAIL if Victor still gets to explain the system in uninterrupted mini-speeches that land as polished maxims.

[chapter_14_f003] PASS if the revised material after `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:349` contains one clear employment consequence, one distinct answer about Dominic's role, and one final Eastbridge beat that changes pressure through image or action; FAIL if those spans still repeat the same machine/cog/system diagnosis without adding a new fact, decision, witness, or consequence.

[F06] Lines 6010-6028 must: (1) not contain narrator-voice restatement of Victor's argument; (2) transition to the Eastbridge drive in 5-8 lines or fewer; (3) end the chapter on the concrete image (Mort in the store, or equivalent) rather than on an abstract realization.

[F14] Lines 5866 and 5898 must: (1) not contain balanced antithetical structures (A is not X. A is Y.); (2) retain Victor's core argument (paper is not the reality, systemic harm has no single throat); (3) include at least one syntactic imperfection — a false start, a subordinate clause that overruns, a practical interruption — that makes Victor sound like a man speaking rather than a writer composing.

findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised language at `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:49`, `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:333`, and `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:391` is recognizably new on the page while preserving the same dramatic function; FAIL if a reader can still point to those spans as verbatim or obvious near-verbatim reuse of style-bible example lines.
findings.problem: Style-bible calibration lines have leaked into the draft verbatim or near-verbatim. Ray's remembered "What the hell was that?", Mort's "They sent a letter.", and Victor's compliment/warning at line 333 all echo protected example-line material instead of generating fresh speech for this chapter.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the leaked language in `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:49`, `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:333`, and `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:391` with new phrasing that preserves the remembered meaning but does not reproduce or closely shadow any `example_lines` from `outline/style_bible.json`. Keep Ray's disgust, Mort's understatement, and Victor's compliment-as-warning, but rebuild each line from this scene's specific pressure rather than the calibration text.

findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised spans at `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:285-321` and `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:389-417` still deliver the corruption facts and job threat but no longer read as a run of back-to-back quotable lines, with at least two visible leverage shifts caused by Nate's pushback or Victor's deflection; FAIL if Victor still gets to explain the system in uninterrupted mini-speeches that land as polished maxims.
findings.problem: Victor's Wednesday scene drifts from dangerous advice into polished explanatory monologue. The confrontation stacks quotable thesis-lines one after another ("The paper is the receipt," the six-signatures speech, the final machine metaphor), so the exchange reads composed rather than overheard and edges toward the villain-speech mode the style bible explicitly forbids.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework Victor's speech in `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:285-321` and `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:389-417` so the same facts arrive through more pressure-shaped conversation: interruptions, partial answers, concrete office-political specifics, and at least one evasive redirect instead of a chain of perfect summations. Keep the required facts about Dreher, the brother-in-law contract, the committed financing, and the employment threat, but reduce the number of clean aphorisms and let Nate force more of the information out of Victor.

findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised material after `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:349` contains one clear employment consequence, one distinct answer about Dominic's role, and one final Eastbridge beat that changes pressure through image or action; FAIL if those spans still repeat the same machine/cog/system diagnosis without adding a new fact, decision, witness, or consequence.
findings.problem: Once the chapter lands its core turn, it keeps re-proving the same thesis. By the time Victor has explained that walking off Eastbridge changes nothing, confirmed Dominic already made the humane case, and tied the issue to Dominic's job, the chapter has already done its central dramatic work. The later office beats and parking-lot aftermath repeatedly restate the same system lesson instead of adding equal new pressure.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or redistribute the material in `snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:349-449`. Preserve three must-land beats only: the employment threat, the answer to why Dominic stayed on the project, and Nate's drive to Eastbridge where he cannot go inside. Any retained sentence in those spans should either sharpen one of those beats with new information or create a fresh physical/emotional consequence; cut or rewrite sentences that merely paraphrase "the system is bigger than one man" in new wording.

findings.acceptance_test: Lines 6010-6028 must: (1) not contain narrator-voice restatement of Victor's argument; (2) transition to the Eastbridge drive in 5-8 lines or fewer; (3) end the chapter on the concrete image (Mort in the store, or equivalent) rather than on an abstract realization.
findings.problem: Overstatement after Victor's devastating verdict. Victor's final assessment of Dominic at lines 5998-6003 ('Because he was good. Because he could make the humane case better than anybody in this office...') is the scene's natural climax. But lines 6010-6028 then reprocess the verdict in interior narration: 'The trap was not abstract anymore' and the drive to Eastbridge recapitulates Victor's argument in reflective summary rather than new action. The reader has already understood what Victor's speech means for Nate; the narrator's re-explanation diminishes Victor's authority by translating his impact into Nate's simpler terms.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:6010-6028, cut the interior reflection to 5-8 lines. Preserve the transition to the car and the drive to Eastbridge (the image of Nate watching Mort through the hardware store window is earned). Cut the narrator's interpretation of what Victor's verdict means — let the preceding dialogue carry its own weight. Specifically, remove or compress any sentence at lines 6012-6018 that restates Victor's argument in Nate's interior voice.

findings.acceptance_test: Lines 5866 and 5898 must: (1) not contain balanced antithetical structures (A is not X. A is Y.); (2) retain Victor's core argument (paper is not the reality, systemic harm has no single throat); (3) include at least one syntactic imperfection — a false start, a subordinate clause that overruns, a practical interruption — that makes Victor sound like a man speaking rather than a writer composing.
findings.problem: Victor's dialogue becomes essay prose. In Chapter 14, Victor delivers multiple compressed aphorisms that read as authorial thesis rather than spoken language: 'Your proposal assumes the paper is the job. It isn't. The paper is the receipt.' (line 5866) and 'They think every bad thing must have one throat you can put your hands around. Mostly it doesn't. Mostly it has six signatures, two favors, a council vote, and a bank that wants what it was promised.' (line 5898). Both are polished to a sheen — balanced antitheses, precisely positioned key words, rhetorical structures designed to land. Victor's established voice (avuncular, indirect, generous with subordinate clauses) can accommodate some polish, but these lines read as composed rather than spoken. When stacked with his Ch16 dialogue, Victor accumulates a disproportionate number of quotable lines that position him as a wisdom-delivery system.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:5866 and 5898: roughen Victor's syntax. The ideas can stay — the novel needs Victor's systemic argument — but the delivery should sound more like a man explaining something to a colleague than an essayist constructing a paragraph. Options: (a) break the balanced structures with hesitation, repetition, or a subordinate clause that wanders before arriving ('The paper is — look, the paper is what's left after the deal happens. It's not the deal.'); (b) have Victor interrupt himself with a practical aside ('You want more coffee? — The paper is the receipt. The deal happened in a room you weren't in.'); (c) let one of the two lines be rough and inarticulate — Victor reaching for an idea he doesn't usually have to explain, finding his usual polish insufficient.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's central premise involves consciousness murder, identity theft, sexual fraud, and the demolition of a working-class community for profit. It must not flinch from any of these. The sex scenes between Nate and Sarah must reckon with the fact that Sarah is being deceived about who is in the bed with her — this is not a comfortable situation and the prose should not make it comfortable. The grief scenes must be physically specific — no 'he was devastated,' only the concrete gestures and sensory details of a man watching his life's work get torn down. The guilt should live in Nate's body (Dominic's body), not in tidy moral reflections. When the novel is funny, it should be funny about things that are actually terrible — the comedy should never feel safe. When Nate discovers that Dominic was trying to help, the reader should feel sick, not enlightened. Risk specificity over tastefulness at every turn.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in place of specificity is a craft failure. When a scene requires difficult content — the moral weight of having killed an innocent man, the complicated ethics of sex under false identity, the physical reality of a man losing his livelihood, the specific humiliation of professional incompetence, the material texture of gambling debts and their enforcement — render it with the same care and specificity given to any other scene. The reader should feel the roughness of the situation, not a smoothed-over summary of it.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or Victor's professional register when delivering serious messages. If a line reads as stiff or literary, check whether contractions would make it sound spoken.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate chapter energy aggressively. A comic workplace chapter should not be followed by another comic workplace chapter. A grief-heavy scene should be followed by something that lets the reader breathe — not through tonal whiplash but through a genuine shift in the kind of pressure being applied. Vary scene counts, dialogue-to-narration ratios, and temporal compression. Some chapters should cover hours; others should cover weeks. At least three chapters should contain extended quiet: meals, walks, conversations about nothing important. These are not filler — they are the human baseline that makes the high-pressure scenes land.
prose_style_profile.diction: Plain American English with a slightly elevated attention to physical specificity. Not minimalist — the prose has room for interiority and texture — but never ornate. Latinate vocabulary only when the concept demands it. Prefer the concrete noun over the abstract one, the specific tool name over the generic 'tool,' the actual menu item over 'food.' The prose should feel like a smart person thinking clearly, not a writer crafting sentences.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, limited to Nate. The camera sits inside Nate's skull — we see through his eyes, think in his rhythms, and register the world through his specific sensory and emotional biases. The narration should reflect Nate's intelligence and his limitations simultaneously: he notices things (sharp observer) but misinterprets them (blinded by resentment, guilt, and self-pity). The narrative should not explain things Nate wouldn't articulate and should not achieve insights Nate hasn't earned.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied sentence length with a center of gravity around 15-20 words. Short declaratives for landing beats. Longer, accumulative sentences for sensory description and interior processing. Avoid sustained staccato (more than three short sentences in sequence) and sustained lyricism (more than two long, clause-heavy sentences in sequence). The prose should breathe.
